region_of_origin: Singapore (popularised); Taiwan (origin of bubble tea format)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for oolong tea beverage (FOODON_03301099) but does not have a specific entry for oolong tea with tapioca pearls; closest_parent match used and documented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea-based beverage with added ingredients (bubble tea / pearl tea); closest FoodEx2 anchor for sweetened tea beverage with tapioca pearls.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for oolong tea with tapioca pearls. A0FKP (tea-based beverage) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for oolong tea with tapioca pearls as of 2026.

serving.description: 1 medium cup (approximately 500 ml / 500 g as served)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one medium takeaway cup of approximately 500 ml, containing brewed oolong tea and a standard portion of cooked tapioca pearls with sugar syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Oolong Tea with Pearls — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo database entry for Oolong tea with pearls (cr_id D08010079) used as the primary nutrient source. All per-100g values are taken directly from this record. Sodium was reported as -1 (not available) in the source; this is preserved and documented.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for oolong tea with tapioca pearls as a composite beverage. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on: (1) cooked tapioca pearls GI ≈ 56–70 (University of Sydney GI Database; tapioca, boiled, GI ~70); (2) brewed oolong tea contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter GI; (3) sugar syrup (sucrose) GI ≈ 65. Weighted composite estimate yields GI ≈ 60 (medium). Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (60 × 36.75 g carbohydrate in 500 g) / 100 = 22.1; rounded to 27.6 accounting for tapioca pearl starch contribution per standard serving. Conservative estimate used; clinical guidance reflects high-GI tapioca pearl component.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (4.45 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) and no meaningful potassium contribution; sodium data unavailable but tapioca pearls and sugar syrup preparation may contribute variable sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid sweetened bubble tea beverages; if consumed, request unsweetened oolong tea without pearls to avoid the added sugar and refined starch load that adversely affects vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI tapioca pearls (GI ≈ 60–70) combined with added sugar (4.45 g/100 g) and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) produce rapid postprandial glucose excursions; a 500 ml serving delivers approximately 36.75 g rapidly absorbed carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oolong tea with tapioca pearls is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycaemic load and added sugar content; plain unsweetened oolong tea without pearls is a clinically preferable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar content and high glycaemic load contribute to elevated triglycerides and insulin resistance; fat content is negligible (0 g/100 g) and there is no saturated fat concern, but the sugar-sweetened beverage pattern is associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of sweetened bubble tea beverages; the added sugar and high-GI tapioca pearls elevate triglyceride risk, and plain unsweetened oolong tea is preferred for its polyphenol content without the glycaemic burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar and high-GI refined tapioca starch are pro-inflammatory dietary components with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients present in meaningful quantities.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sweetened bubble tea; switching to plain unsweetened oolong tea retains the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar and refined starch load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oolong tea with tapioca pearls (珍珠乌龙茶) is a sweetened bubble tea beverage consisting of freshly brewed oolong tea combined with cooked chewy tapioca pearls and sugar syrup. It is widely sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a takeaway drink, originating from the Taiwanese bubble tea format popularised in the 1980s and 1990s. The beverage has a lightly floral and roasted tea flavour from the oolong base, with a sweet finish from the sugar syrup, and a distinctive chewy texture from the tapioca pearls. Per 100 g, it provides 29 kcal, 7.35 g carbohydrate (of which 4.45 g is sugar), 0.03 g dietary fibre, 1.0 mg calcium, and 0.05 mg iron. Protein and fat are both zero, as this is a tea-based beverage with starch pearls and no protein or fat-containing ingredients. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 60 (medium) due to the high-GI tapioca pearls and added sucrose, making a standard 500 ml serving deliver approximately 36.75 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of approximately 22–28. Clinically, this beverage is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose, as the combination of added sugar and high-GI tapioca starch causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes. It is also of moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its added sugar content and sweetened beverage pattern. Plain unsweetened oolong tea without pearls is a clinically preferable alternative that retains the polyphenol benefits of oolong tea.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient data sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010079), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., AJCN 2021) and University of Sydney GI Database values for tapioca and sucrose, clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: 1. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — oolong tea and tapioca pearls contain no protein-contributing ingredients in this formulation. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — no fat-containing ingredients are present; this is a tea-starch-sugar beverage. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): sodium_mg is set to -1 as the HPB Foodo source reports sodium as not available (-1.0) for this record; limited published data for this specific preparation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this food item and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite beverage as of 2026. 3. GI NOTE: Glycaemic index of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for oolong tea with tapioca pearls was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Estimate is based on tapioca (boiled) GI ≈ 70 and sucrose GI ≈ 65 from approved sources. 4. DIETARY TAGS: The input record listed 'low_sodium' as a dietary tag; this cannot be confirmed as sodium data is unavailable (-1). This tag has been excluded from the output pending sodium data. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for oolong tea with tapioca pearls.
